title: Plain-weave floor loom sampler
subtitle: A beginner sampler on a four-shaft floor loom, covering winding the warp, threading, sleying, and weaving a balanced plain weave
excerpt: A sampler is the best first project on a floor loom: it teaches every stage of setting up the loom and produces a useful piece of cloth without requiring a complex threading or treadling sequence. This entry covers winding a warp, threading heddles, sleying the reed, and weaving a balanced plain weave from start to finish.
sourceNotes: Floor loom plain weave sampler synthesised from general four-shaft loom setup practice. Plain weave is the simplest balanced weave structure and the standard starting point for loom weaving instruction.

glossaryTerms.definition: The set of threads tensioned lengthways on the loom. The warp provides the structural skeleton through which the weft interlaces.
The thread carried horizontally across the warp on each pick, interlacing alternately over and under the warp ends.
The number of warp ends per centimetre or per inch, determined by the reed dent count. The sett controls how open or dense the finished cloth is.
The side edges of the woven cloth where the weft turns back on itself at each pick. Consistent selvedges require consistent tension on the weft as it enters the shed.
The triangular opening formed between raised and lowered warp threads when a treadle is pressed. The shuttle passes through the shed to lay the weft pick.

aliases: four shaft plain weave loom setup

content.text: A four-shaft floor loom can weave complex patterns. The most useful first project is plain weave, where the

ends on every pick. A sampler lets you practise each stage of loom setup and weaving without the pressure of producing a perfect piece, and the finished cloth is a record of what you learned. Use a smooth, medium-weight wool or cotton yarn at a
appropriate to the yarn diameter: for a DK-weight wool, 6-8 ends per cm is a good starting point.

items.name: Warp yarn, smooth wool or cotton, DK weight
Weft yarn, same or complementary, DK weight
Four-shaft floor loom, at least 40 cm weaving width

content.text: Calculate the number of warp ends needed: weaving width (in cm) multiplied by sett (ends per cm). For a 30 cm wide sampler at 7 ends per cm, wind 210 ends. On the warping board, wind figure-of-eight crosses at the start and end of each chain to maintain the threading order. Keep each pass at identical tension by winding with a consistent pull. The finished warp chain should be 100-120 cm longer than the planned weaving length to allow for loom waste.

For plain weave on four shafts, thread in a straight draw: end 1 through shaft 1, end 2 through shaft 2, end 3 through shaft 1, end 4 through shaft 2, and so on. Only shafts 1 and 2 are used for plain weave; shafts 3 and 4 remain empty. Thread from the cross in the warp chain to maintain the correct order. Use a threading hook to pull each end through the heddle eye.

Choose a reed with a dent count that achieves your planned sett. For 7 ends per cm with a 7-dent reed, sley one end per dent. Use the reed hook to draw each warp end through the appropriate dent from back to front. Work from the centre of the reed outward to keep the warp centred in the loom. Once all ends are sleyed, tie them to the front beam in small bundles of 10-12 ends, adjusting until the tension is even across the full width.
Press treadle 1 (shaft 1 up) to open the first
. Pass the shuttle through and let the weft arc slightly before beating: this extra length prevents the weft from pulling in the

. Beat once with the beater, then press treadle 2 (shaft 2 up) and pass the shuttle back. Alternate treadles for every pick. Check selvedge tension after every 10 picks: the warp width in the weaving should match the width in the reed.

items.problem: The weaving narrows significantly after the first few picks
items.cause: The weft was pulled too tightly before beating, drawing the selvedge ends inward
items.fix: Let the weft arc across the shed before beating. The arc provides the extra length needed to weave in without narrowing the cloth.

items.problem: The shed is not clean: some warp ends lie in the wrong position
items.cause: Threading errors placed some ends on the wrong shaft
items.fix: Check the threading sequence from the beginning. A threading error at end N shows as a float on every other pick from that point. Correct by moving the offending end to the correct heddle.

items.problem: The warp tension is uneven across the width
items.cause: The warp was not wound at consistent tension on the warping board, or the front beam tie-on bundles were not adjusted
items.fix: Release the front beam bundles, re-tension by pulling each bundle to an even firmness, and re-tie. Uneven tension is easier to correct before weaving begins than after.
